author: ext3h
content: No, there is probably a real bug in there, some sort of use-after-free, which only is noticeable in Debug mode due to debug heap features. Not entirely sure what's happening, but most likely an issue triggered by the implicit sharing mechanism.

author: ext3h
content: Write access in `AggregatorInventory::updateWatchedItem` caused iterator invalidation in the outer loop, due to an already in-flight 2nd reference to `GameState::watchedItemList` somwhere else

author: ext3h
content: And that 2nd reference came from `signalWatchList` inside `updateWatchedItem` during the previous outer iteration.

author: rsmith32
content: I think I get what your saying. Just to clarify  AggregatorInventory::updateWatchedItem  is causing an element in GameState::watchedItemList to be modified or deleted in a way that when it returns to the previous function AggregatorInventory::init  which is still currently iterating over the GameState::watchedItemList to get an invalid reference.

author: rsmith32
ext3h
content: Why is `m_squad` a `QList` in the first place? That's bound to break as soon as you ever delete a squad???

author: ext3h
content: About right. If this was an STL container, it would require an insert or erase to invalidate iterators. In the case of Qt, every non-const access to the contained data can cause invalidation. Accidental copy of containers with mutable content is quite dangerous in Qt.

author: rsmith32
ext3h
content: Depends whether they are indexed by squad ID, or if they are only plain lists.

author: rsmith32
content: <@!406873466537508864> just realized I had totally messed up my pull request from a few days ago. I fixed it and re-requested.

author: rsmith32
content: Also, curious what your thoughts are on making the object class extend from QObject. We would be able to implicitly do what were currently doing with the CreatureType enum. We would also be able to write better generics in general. I know QObjects arn't light but I think it would still be worth it. I work with unreal engine and use its reflection system quite heavily.  I'm also making the assumption that QTs reflection system is similar to Unreals.

author: ext3h
content: QObject comes with quite a lot of overhead, and you are not exactly supposed to have hundred thousands of them alive concurrently. If you aim at the "reflections" provided by the property system in Qt, you even have to go the route via string based lookups at runtime, nothing you would actually want to do.

author: ext3h
content: Unless I'm mistaken, part of what makes the `CreatureType` enum currently "necessary", is that the inheritance structure in the whole creatures tree wasn't exactly fully thought through.   Like the distinction between "monster" and "animal", that should not even exist in the first place. The behavior tree should differ, but not the C++ type.

author: ext3h
content: And it used a type hierarchy where it should have used entity-component pattern

author: ext3h
content: E.g. components to plug in the specialized anatomy (damage, needs/upkeep, reproduction), movement constraints, can-work extension, combat-/social-stance (squad based, monster squad, ant swarm, regular animal).

author: rsmith32
content:
Qts reflection system uses strings for runtime lookups? yikes.  Unreal provides a pointer to a class type object. After I typed that out I also saw that items extended from object as well which your totally right would be way to heavy for all of them to be QObjects.  I agree the component pattern should definitely be something to move towards. Again though you would still need some kind of reflection to grab a type of component otherwise your writing a function for each component and/or need an enum to check for the correct type to access it.  
```
//something that could look like this and be one generic function
getComponent<CanMove>()
getComponent<damage>()

//will look like this
GetCanMoveComponent()
GetDamageComponent()

//Another example with but using an enum as type. but this would be unsafe as passing in wrong types would cause an incorrect runtime cast
getComponent<CanMoveComponent>(ComponentType::CanMove)
```
I'm doing something similiar in the pull requrest I just made. 

You could also use dynamic_casts and get similar results, you could have a generic function that could  look like getComponent<CanMove>() but dynamic_casts are fairly slow if your doing a bunch of them. And unnecessary if your already storing an enum type with the class.

author: ext3h
content: There is no need to use a generic base class for components though, is there? It's not like we would have to design an entity-component *framework*, but we can use typed member pointers.

author: rsmith32
rsmith32
content: Also, even though i said its not hard to implement it still would take time to refactor the existing code to work like this. I think giving them a generic base component and then making them members variables like you said is the best first step.

author: arcnor
content:
few things that you might or might not like:
1. This first set of changes just removes `QSet` completely, because it was the easiest
2. I've changed the C++ version to C++20, because the STL has improved a lot on that version, it makes things easier without extra libs. This can be reverted to 17, but things will have to be changed (for this first step, `std::set` will not have a `contains()` anymore)
3. Because of this, Linux now compiles with GCC 10 (instead of the previous GCC 7)

author: ext3h
content: Beware of some performance differences between STL and the Qt containers. Copying (nested) Qt structures at the message queue boundary is a lazy, shallow copy-on-write. With the STL containers it's an eager deep copy.

author: ext3h
content: Also found the performance of `std:: unordered_*` containers to be lacking compared to the Qt ones - need to switch over to `absl::flat_hash_*` in some places.

author: arcnor
content: Yeah, this is just a first pass, it's easier using the STL, but then performance analysis can be done, should be easy to change to specific ones later

author: ext3h
content: The water update algorithm is the most perf critical part where the STL containers failed to do the job. Recording tile updates also somewhat. Everything else should be mostly fine.

author: arcnor
content: Cool, should be easy to profile

content: biggest issue with replacing the sigslot stuff is that thread safety is not guaranteed, because the library obviously doesn't know anything about Qt threads, so things like `QueuedConnection` cannot be done automatically. I'm going to replace things, then worry about these issues later, getting everything to compile is hard enough for now 😄

author: arcnor

author: ext3h
content: Hmm, will need to take a look at threading later. Because thread affinity is a huge part of the queued connection thing.

author: arcnor
content: the idea basically is replacing that with either manual mutexing of data inside the slots/callbacks, or replicating what Qt does when using a `QueuedConnection` (which AFAIK is message passing, and probably serializing the arguments somehow as well to make that work)

author: ext3h
content: Correct, it's message passing. And we should try to keep it that way, as the backend would otherwise stall the UI.

author: arcnor
content: yeah, it's stalling in a few cases now

author: ext3h
content: When establishing a queued connection, we would need to specify the queue to target

author: ext3h
content: Unlike Qt, we don't need the ability to change affinity after construction of an object

author: ext3h
content: And all objects have a non-ambiguous affinity which can be derived by their type, so we can decide statically whether to model a specific connection as message or callback.

author: ext3h
content: We do have a mix of messages/callbacks for each signal/slot though.

author: ext3h
content: Qt didn't require serialization, and technically neither do we for now. We only have to refactor for parameter packing, and then we can emplace the message in a smart pointer managed object when a queued connection exists.

content: There is a chance a message for the UI is generated from within the UI thread. Although I suppose it would be easier to handle if we did strictly distinguish between message passing and callbacks.

content: You haven't seen it, because I think we eliminated it entirely, only the aggregators use message passing, everything else is synchronous.

content:
```cmake
if(UNIX AND NOT (DEFINED ENV{CXXFLAGS} OR CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS))
    set(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS "-Wall -Wextra -fpermissive  -g3 -ggdb")
    # Kill some overly verbose warnings
    set(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS "${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS} -Wno-unknown-pragmas -Wno-unused-parameter")
    # But treat likely errors as actual errors
    set(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS "${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS} -Werror=missing-field-initializers -Werror=ignored-qualifiers -Werror=delete-non-virtual-dtor -Werror=unused-value")
endif()

if(MSVC)
    set(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS "${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS} /Gy /Gw /Oi /MP /GS- /W3 /permissive- /await:strict /Zc:__cplusplus /Zc:rvalueCast /Zc:strictStrings /Zc:referenceBinding /Zc:ternary /Zi /w24458 /w34456 /w24702")
    set(CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS "${CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS} /CGTHREADS:8 /DEBUG /INCREMENTAL:NO /OPT:REF /OPT:ICF")
    set(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELWITHDEBINFO "${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELWITHDEBINFO} /Ob2")
endif()
```

author: ext3h
content:
Pondering what to use for the message queue...
Leaning towards  `std::unique_ptr<std::function<void(void)>>` as the base container template, but still pondering how much flexibility I should account for with regard to coupling specific sender/receiver pairs.

As long as they are pairwise, I believe I can just bind the receiver directly from the senders thread, and I can completely forgo serialization for now?

Just need to make sure the sender provides data to `std::bind` strictly as rvalues, and the receiver works strictly with lvalue references.

author: ext3h
content: I don't think we will ever need n:1 or 1:n connections on the message passing end.

author: ext3h
content: The receiving end requires a base class for providing push access to the correct queue....

author: ext3h
content: Got to be careful to only set up signal/slot connections after construction in the correct thread so not to risk any races.

author: ext3h
content: And in reverse also the detail about destruction in the correct thread - which mustn't be on the sender but on the receivers end - after the sender discards it's (only?) strong ref.

author: ext3h
content: The sender can't own the receiver directly, it can at most own a reference counter. On expiring, full ownership needs to be transferred to the target queue, and needs to be released there.

author: ext3h
content: The construction should already happen in the correct thread as well... However, if I were to use a future here to obtain the receiver object for binding, there is a risk of deadlocks.

author: ext3h
content: Damn, not that easy to just replace the comfort Qt gave:/

author: ext3h
content: Maybe protobuf and going for a generic buffer only interface between sender and receiver with a factory service (including remote destruction) on the simulation side is easier to implement. I don't think I'm going to be happy for long with having to manage the corresponding other threads pointers.

author: ext3h
content: Factory service which doubles as router also reliefs me of the question where the injected aggregators were supposed to get the simulation objects from for setting up their connections.

author: ext3h
content: With a factory service, the UI needs to specify a unique topic for distinction...

author: ext3h
content:
```
# For a list of common variables see https://github.com/microsoft/vcpkg/blob/master/docs/maintainers/vcpkg_common_definitions.md

# Download source packages
# (bgfx requires bx and bimg source for building)

vcpkg_from_github(OUT_SOURCE_PATH BX_SOURCE_PATH
    REPO "bkaradzic/bx"
    HEAD_REF master
    REF aa5090bbd8c39e84d483f4850a7d9ca7ff9241ed
    SHA512 7b95ff924ee22bbda67783ad06e37869c24e6d1cc67bcac37deb0998398d26c83af737c8b8e4c6cde07d785d80f70157e197549a6b2041cde46793fe8ca30efc
)

vcpkg_from_github(OUT_SOURCE_PATH BIMG_SOURCE_PATH
    REPO "bkaradzic/bimg"
    HEAD_REF master
    REF 663f724186e26caf46494e389ed82409106205fb
    SHA512 6fcd6d06d0d55a8f573a882bb57b729ee22101314a201bd8f440bdcadbfeb0dca6834302ef7dcfdee785d87b5456d24d2cb630d1d39836b4cd7652a6dc92d5a8
)

vcpkg_from_github(OUT_SOURCE_PATH BGFX_SOURCE_PATH
    REPO "bkaradzic/bgfx"
    HEAD_REF master
    REF db44d5675f4e02a9f3178fe54ac6d5a9c216a573
    SHA512 207b7fcd10a0d1205984a2ef2360ff410d4aa37b7f87e4bd0562cbedaecd87ef1666746035cb99ad8614cea8abd57e467e0a1ffda9f5b7266351e58a9e2fff5d
)

vcpkg_from_github(OUT_SOURCE_PATH SOURCE_PATH
    REPO "bkaradzic/bgfx.cmake"
    HEAD_REF master
    REF 12b75cc0ad0078a700d4854db16e119ef4706347
    SHA512 35e0d84cd427f34036d0ab6404bdd90147f36bb0483cbc575107a6e2ed77a150bf943858db9bd8a091a1a6a02d3821d4d93181fa5997507686d34a8ecc22a318
)

set(ROOT ${CURRENT_PACKAGES_DIR}/bgfx.cmake)
file(MAKE_DIRECTORY "${ROOT}")
file(MAKE_DIRECTORY "${ROOT}/bx")
file(MAKE_DIRECTORY "${ROOT}/bimg")
file(MAKE_DIRECTORY "${ROOT}/bgfx")
file(COPY "${SOURCE_PATH}/" DESTINATION "${ROOT}")
file(COPY "${BX_SOURCE_PATH}/" DESTINATION "${ROOT}/bx")
file(COPY "${BIMG_SOURCE_PATH}/" DESTINATION "${ROOT}/bimg")
file(COPY "${BGFX_SOURCE_PATH}/" DESTINATION "${ROOT}/bgfx")

vcpkg_cmake_configure(
    SOURCE_PATH "${ROOT}"
    DISABLE_PARALLEL_CONFIGURE
)

vcpkg_cmake_install()
vcpkg_fixup_pkgconfig()
vcpkg_copy_pdbs()

file(REMOVE_RECURSE "${ROOT}")
```

author: ext3h
content:
For the most part by forking https://github.com/rschurade/Ingnomia on Github, and the opening a pull request for your contributions. As for what you can do? Partially depends on what skills you already have, but also what's needed. Tickets with the "help wanted" label in the bug tracker are essentially "free for all". Usually it would be a good idea though to ask in this channel to make sure the feature isn't already being worked on by someone else.

The build instructions listed in the repositories readme are still valid for now, even though we are currently swapping out the dependency management.

author: ext3h
content: I think we should use the chance to clean up the sprite definition a little bit. There is some really weird details I don't like, such as `SpriteSeason` having a nested `SpriteSeasonRotation`, but that missing a `Sprite` key e.g. Or `BaseSprite` / `Sprite` not being strictly `oneof` within the different compositions. Some dead flags, such as `Sprite::Rot90`, `Sprite::Anim`, `Sprite::HasRandom` or `Sprite::HasTransp` which should had been derived from the actual content / sprite hierarchy.

author: ext3h
content: `Effect: "none"` in `SpriteByMaterials::Effect` indicates a missing optional

author: ext3h
content: Valid effects should be an enum, `Rot90, FlipHorizontal`.

author: clawthorn
content: Remember that if you choose to go the vibe coding route instead of actually learning and understanding, you will end up in situations where you have a problem that needs to be solved, but you cannot solve it because the AI keeps going around in circles not doing what you tell it to do. It's happened a thousand times before to people who built entire SaaS'es around vibe coding, and it will happen to you.

author: ald_productions
content: I will also caution that you have blind spots, and Claud WILL put bugs in them while not helping you descover them.

author: mugofjoe
content: I’m aware of what comes with relying on AI to do intensive work. I respect the input but im more so looking to see if anyone is doing similar.

author: clawthorn
content:
I don't think you'll find anyone in here. The server is pretty inactive, and from what I've seen the gamedev (and gamer) community as a whole leans heavily against AI. As does pretty much every creative community. So you'd have much more luck trying some reddit or discord community dedicated specifically for AI. Like ones for OpenClaw or Google Genie or r/vibecoding.
As for the art assets, there are plenty of tutorials on local GenAI in the Stable Diffusion discord (and the Furry Diffusion one especially). And Aseprite also have several local AI plugins to let you generate and work with sprites (like [PixelAI](https://red335.itch.io/pixelai-local-ai-directly-in-aseprite) or [Retro Diffusion](https://astropulse.itch.io/retrodiffusion)).
